planting.note: Direct sow after last frost once soil reaches 65°F; in zone 9 this is typically February–March for spring and again August–September for a productive fall crop. Purple hull peas thrive in high heat and humidity and are the classic summer legume of the Gulf Coast.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep in any well-drained soil in full sun; purple hull peas are exceptionally drought and heat tolerant once established and require no added fertilizer on moderately fertile ground since they fix atmospheric nitrogen. The distinctive purple-hull pods flush violet-pink to purple at maturity, making them easy to spot at harvest time — pick pods when the hull is fully colored but before it begins to dry and yellow. Fresh-shelled purple hull peas cook faster than dried beans and have a richer, earthier flavor than black-eyed peas; they are a staple of Southern cooking, particularly New Year's Day hoppin' john and summer side dishes. The vines sprawl moderately — plant in wide rows or support with short stakes for easier harvest.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Southern Pea Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Southern Pea
Alabama Cooperative Extension System — Growing Southern Peas

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; in zone 9 plant March–May for spring harvest and August–September for fall. Whippoorwill is a sprawling, vigorous heirloom that does best with room to spread; allow 36–48 inches between rows.

directions: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep in well-drained soil and allow vines to sprawl freely; Whippoorwill is a pre-Civil War Southern heirloom with a sprawling, semi-vining habit that naturalizes well in field conditions. The speckled brown-and-cream seeds are distinctive and historically used both as a fresh shell pea and a dry bean, with a rich, slightly nutty flavor preferred by many Southern gardeners over modern varieties. It is particularly favored for field cultivation as a dual-purpose food and cover crop in the South, where it fixes substantial nitrogen and suppresses summer weeds. Seeds are saved easily — allow selected pods to dry fully on the vine.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Whippoorwill Pea
Seed Savers Exchange — Heirloom Southern Peas
Alabama Cooperative Extension System — Southern Pea Varieties

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; eggplant needs sustained warmth and benefits from bottom heat during germination. Transplant in zone 9 February–March for a long growing season; eggplant thrives in the heat and often continues producing through October–November.

directions: Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Pingtung Long is a Taiwanese heirloom eggplant that produces slender, 12–18 inch fruits in a vibrant lavender-purple, among the longest eggplant fruits available. The skin is exceptionally thin and tender with minimal bitterness, making it prized for quick stir-fries and grilling without peeling. Plants are productive and heat-tolerant, continuing to set fruit through the hot zone 9 summers when other eggplant varieties may pause. Harvest fruits young when skin is glossy and before seeds develop; overripe fruits become seedy and bitter. A heavy producer that may need light staking to support the long, heavy fruits.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Pingtung Long Eggplant
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Eggplant Production in Florida
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Pingtung Long

planting.note: Start indoors 6 weeks before last frost; transplant in zone 9 February–March for spring production. Sunmaster is specifically bred to set fruit at temperatures above 90°F when most varieties drop blossoms, making it one of the best heat-set tomatoes for the Gulf Coast.

directions: Transplant into deeply amended, well-drained soil with a sturdy cage or stake; Sunmaster is an indeterminate heat-set tomato developed by the University of Florida specifically for production in hot, humid climates where daytime temperatures regularly exceed 90°F. Unlike many tomato varieties that drop blossoms at high temperatures, Sunmaster sets fruit reliably even when nights stay above 75°F, giving zone 9 gardeners a productive window from early May through June and again in September–November from fall plantings. The large, smooth, red globe fruits average 8–10 ounces with good flavor and crack resistance. Consistent watering is essential — irregular moisture leads to blossom-end rot and fruit cracking in the summer heat.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Production for Florida
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Tomatoes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tomato Varieties for Louisiana

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant February–March for spring and again August for a productive fall crop. Rattlesnake is one of the most heat-tolerant pole beans and continues setting beans through summer heat that stops most other varieties.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep at the base of a sturdy trellis, tepee, or fence — rattlesnake beans are vigorous climbers reaching 8–10 feet tall. This 19th-century Southern heirloom is named for its distinctive pods, which are green with distinctive purple streaking that resembles snakeskin; the streaks fade to plain green when cooked. It is one of the most heat-tolerant pole beans available, continuing to set pods through the June–July Gulf Coast heat that causes most beans to stop producing. Harvest young pods at 6–8 inches for snap beans; the mature seeds are purple-speckled tan and make excellent dried beans with a rich, full flavor. The plants are ornamental on a trellis or arbor.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Rattlesnake Bean
Seed Savers Exchange — Rattlesnake Pole Bean
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bean Production in Florida
